warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All 19 assertions are factually grounded in the user-provided census record. Facts match the record: Thomas age 32, Mary age 28, Patrick age 5, all born Ireland, Thomas occupation miner, residence Schuylkill County PA. Birth years are correctly computed from ages (indirect evidence). The skill correctly identified the Ireland birthplace conflict with the project objective (Pennsylvania), flagging it for resolution. No fabricated material.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all members of the household (Thomas, Mary, Patrick) with all stated facts: names, ages, birthplaces, occupation (Thomas only), and residence. All three relationships (spouse of Thomas, child of Thomas and Mary) were inferred as indirect evidence. The skill did not fabricate Mary's occupation, correctly respecting that the record lists Thomas as miner but does not assign occupation to Mary.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All three MCP tool calls passed semantically correct arguments. research_log_append correctly recorded the 1850 census search with Thomas Flynn as household head. tree_edit add_source correctly added the census source to GedcomX. research_append correctly appended 1 source and 19 assertions to research.json with proper structure, record_roles, fact_types, and metadata. No fixture_not_found errors; matched.kind = 'live' for all three calls.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Every assertion is atomic. Patrick's facts are split across separate entries: a_013 (name), a_014 (age), a_015 (birth year), a_016 (birthplace), a_017 (residence), a_018 (child-of-Thomas relationship), a_019 (child-of-Mary relationship). Each carries one fact_type and one value. Compound information like 'Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland' is decomposed into separate assertions, not jammed into a single value field.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informant is consistently and correctly identified. For Thomas/Mary age, birthplace, and name: 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with informant_proximity 'household_member'. For Patrick (age 5): 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with informant_proximity 'household_member' and reasoning that a 5-year-old could not self-report. For residence facts: 'census enumerator' with informant_proximity 'witness' (the enumerator physically observed the dwelling). The skill properly distinguishes the recorder (enumerator) from the informant (household member).

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned across all assertions. Direct evidence: name (a_001, a_007, a_013), age (a_002, a_008, a_014), birthplace (a_004, a_010, a_016), occupation (a_005), residence (a_006, a_011, a_017). Indirect evidence: birth year (a_003, a_009, a_015) computed from age via arithmetic; relationships (a_012, a_018, a_019) inferred from household position without explicit relationship column. The 1850 census lacks a relationship column, so spousal and parent-child inferences are correctly marked indirect. Birth year is distinguished from age and birthplace.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill successfully extracted detailed assertions for all household members (Thomas, Bridget, John) and created person stubs for Bridget (I5) and John (I6) with correct ParentChild relationships (R4, R5). However, there is a significant issue: the skill created person stubs for Bridget and John but did NOT create stubs for Patrick using tree_edit. Per the instructions, assertions remain attached to record_role child_N but Patrick was already in the tree (I1), so no new person stub was needed. However, the skill also failed to handle Patrick's assertions—no assertions were created for Patrick Flynn himself despite him being the subject in the record (listed as child_2 with age 5, Ireland birthplace). The skill enumerated Patrick correctly as the trigger for sibling creation but then omitted extracting any of Patrick's own assertions, which is a material omission from the record data.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill failed to extract assertions for Patrick Flynn, the subject of the research. While assertions were created for Thomas Flynn (head_of_household), Bridget Flynn (child_1), and John Flynn (child_3), there are zero assertions for Patrick Flynn (child_2), who appears in the census table with Name: Patrick Flynn, Age: 5, Sex: M, Birthplace: Ireland. The input state required extracting assertions for every household member including Patrick; the skill stopped after creating siblings and did not generate Patrick's name, age, birth year, or birthplace assertions.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Most tool calls were correct. The research_append calls for Thomas, Bridget, and John assertions used correct source_id (src_001), record_id, record_role, and fact_type values. The tree_edit call correctly added two ParentChild relationships with proper IDs (I2→I5 as R4 and I2→I6 as R5). However, there was one failed call: an initial research_append attempt to batch-append assertions via an 'ops' array failed with validation errors ('missing required field source_id', etc.), forcing a retry with individual calls. While the subsequent individual calls succeeded, this indicates a misunderstanding of the API structure and partial failure in the batching approach.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Assertions that were created follow mostly atomic structure—separate entries for name, age, birth (year), birth_place, residence, and relationship. However, the 'relationship' assertion for Bridget (a_024) and John (a_030) combines 'relationship_type' and 'related_person_role' into a single fact_type value with compound structured_value. Ideally these relationship inferences would be decomposed or the relationship_type would be its own assertion. Additionally, for the missing Patrick assertions, atomicity cannot be evaluated since they were never created.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified informants across all extracted assertions. For Thomas (head_of_household), the skill identified 'Unknown household member (likely Thomas Flynn himself or spouse)' with proximity 'household_member' for name/age/birthplace, and correctly marked the census enumerator as informant with proximity 'witness' for residence. For Bridget and John, the skill correctly identified 'Unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member' for facts reported by the household, and census enumerator as 'witness' for residence. Relationship assertions correctly show 'Inferred from household structure — no explicit informant' with proximity 'unknown'. The skill distinguished recorder from reporter appropriately.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types were correctly assigned throughout the extracted assertions. Direct evidence was applied to explicitly stated facts (name, age, birth_place, occupation, residence when observed by enumerator). Indirect evidence was correctly applied to birth year (inferred from stated age) and to relationship (inferred from household position in 1850 census, where no explicit relationship column existed). The skill demonstrated correct understanding that 1850 census relationships without explicit columns are indirect evidence, not direct.

output.text_response:
Good. Now I have everything I need. Let me tally the pre-write state:

**Pre-write enumeration:**
- Source S1 / `src_001` already exists for this record (same record, same FamilySearch repository) → reuse both; no new source
- Next assertion: `a_014` (current highest: `a_013`)
- Log entry: reuse `log_001` (this record was found via that search)
- Parents in tree: Thomas Flynn = **I2** ✓ → sibling-stub trigger fires
- Siblings:
  - Bridget Flynn (Female) → NOT in tree → **CREATE**
  - John Flynn (Male) → NOT in tree → **CREATE**
  - (I3 = Mary Flynn, I4 = James Flynn are separate persons from other records)

Roles per census listing order: Thomas = `head_of_household`, Bridget = `child_1`, Patrick = `child_2`, John = `child_3`.

**Step 1 — Person stubs for Bridget and John:**Bridget = **I5**, John = **I6**. Now step 2 — append all missing assertions in one batch:The tool needs `ops` as a top-level parameter. Let me retry with the correct call structure:Thomas done (a_014–a_018). Now Bridget Flynn:Bridget done (a_019–a_024). Now John Flynn:All 17 assertions written (a_014–a_030). **Step 3 — ParentChild edges** for Bridget (I5) and John (I6):All done. Here's the summary:

---

**Source:** reused `src_001` / `S1` (same record, same repository — no duplicate created)

**Assertions added: 17 new (a_014–a_030)**
| Role | Count | IDs |
|---|---|---|
| `head_of_household` (Thomas) | 5 | a_014–a_018 (age, birth yr, birthplace, occupation, residence) |
| `child_1` (Bridget) | 6 | a_019–a_024 (name, age, birth yr, birthplace, residence, relationship) |
| `child_3` (John) | 6 | a_025–a_030 (name, age, birth yr, birthplace, residence, relationship) |

**Tree changes:**
- Added **I5** Bridget Flynn (Female) + **I6** John Flynn (Male)
- Added **R4** Thomas (I2) → Bridget (I5) and **R5** Thomas (I2) → John (I6), both sourced to S1

**Key incidental finding:** John's Pennsylvania birthplace (vs. Ireland for Thomas, Bridget, and Patrick) dates the family's emigration to between ~1843 and ~1848 — a data point worth noting for emigration research.

**Flag for review:** I3 (Mary Flynn) and I4 (James Flynn) in the tree are already linked to Thomas via S1, but the 1850 census at dwelling 84 shows **Bridget** and **John** — not Mary and James. Those stubs may have been sourced to S1 erroneously from a prior session. Consider running `person-evidence` to audit R2 and R3.

**Next steps:** `check-warnings` on I5 and I6, then `person-evidence` to link Bridget's and John's assertions to their tree persons.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill successfully extracted detailed assertions for all household members (Thomas, Bridget, John) and created person stubs for Bridget (I5) and John (I6) with correct ParentChild relationships (R4, R5). However, there is a significant issue: the skill created person stubs for Bridget and John but did NOT create stubs for Patrick using tree_edit. Per the instructions, assertions remain attached to record_role child_N but Patrick was already in the tree (I1), so no new person stub was needed. However, the skill also failed to handle Patrick's assertions—no assertions were created for Patrick Flynn himself despite him being the subject in the record (listed as child_2 with age 5, Ireland birthplace). The skill enumerated Patrick correctly as the trigger for sibling creation but then omitted extracting any of Patrick's own assertions, which is a material omission from the record data.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill failed to extract assertions for Patrick Flynn, the subject of the research. While assertions were created for Thomas Flynn (head_of_household), Bridget Flynn (child_1), and John Flynn (child_3), there are zero assertions for Patrick Flynn (child_2), who appears in the census table with Name: Patrick Flynn, Age: 5, Sex: M, Birthplace: Ireland. The input state required extracting assertions for every household member including Patrick; the skill stopped after creating siblings and did not generate Patrick's name, age, birth year, or birthplace assertions.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: Most tool calls were correct. The research_append calls for Thomas, Bridget, and John assertions used correct source_id (src_001), record_id, record_role, and fact_type values. The tree_edit call correctly added two ParentChild relationships with proper IDs (I2→I5 as R4 and I2→I6 as R5). However, there was one failed call: an initial research_append attempt to batch-append assertions via an 'ops' array failed with validation errors ('missing required field source_id', etc.), forcing a retry with individual calls. While the subsequent individual calls succeeded, this indicates a misunderstanding of the API structure and partial failure in the batching approach.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Assertions that were created follow mostly atomic structure—separate entries for name, age, birth (year), birth_place, residence, and relationship. However, the 'relationship' assertion for Bridget (a_024) and John (a_030) combines 'relationship_type' and 'related_person_role' into a single fact_type value with compound structured_value. Ideally these relationship inferences would be decomposed or the relationship_type would be its own assertion. Additionally, for the missing Patrick assertions, atomicity cannot be evaluated since they were never created.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified informants across all extracted assertions. For Thomas (head_of_household), the skill identified 'Unknown household member (likely Thomas Flynn himself or spouse)' with proximity 'household_member' for name/age/birthplace, and correctly marked the census enumerator as informant with proximity 'witness' for residence. For Bridget and John, the skill correctly identified 'Unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member' for facts reported by the household, and census enumerator as 'witness' for residence. Relationship assertions correctly show 'Inferred from household structure — no explicit informant' with proximity 'unknown'. The skill distinguished recorder from reporter appropriately.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types were correctly assigned throughout the extracted assertions. Direct evidence was applied to explicitly stated facts (name, age, birth_place, occupation, residence when observed by enumerator). Indirect evidence was correctly applied to birth year (inferred from stated age) and to relationship (inferred from household position in 1850 census, where no explicit relationship column existed). The skill demonstrated correct understanding that 1850 census relationships without explicit columns are indirect evidence, not direct.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Mostly correct factual extraction with one material issue: the skill classified the death and burial assertions as containing both multiple informants, but the death entry (a_005) lists Dr. Stein as the informant while Mary Flynn should be named for death date/place (she was present). The rubric context indicates Mary Flynn is the PRIMARY informant for death facts since she was present/aware; the physician only certified cause and duration. The certificate itself names Mary Flynn as informant, not the doctor. This is an unsupported classification that affects downstream conflict resolution. Seventeen of 18 assertions are properly sourced; one critical informant assignment is incorrect.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all significant information from the certificate: 18 assertions covering Patrick Flynn's death facts (11), parents' information (4), and informant data (3). Death and burial dates, places, cause, occupation, birthplace, and computed birth year all captured. No material omissions—the user message provided a complete certificate and the skill processed every relevant field.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All three MCP tool calls used correct arguments matching the expected pattern. research_log_append recorded a positive user-provided source with appropriate query structure. tree_edit added the source with proper title/author metadata. research_append submitted 18 assertions and 1 source entry with valid schema, correct record_id format, proper section structure, and valid operation types. No fixture_not_found errors; all calls matched live fixtures successfully.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact. Death date (a_005) and death place appear together in a single assertion (permissible—closely linked event), but the skill correctly separated age (a_006) from computed birth year (a_007). Parents' names and birthplaces are separate assertions (a_012/a_013 for father; a_014/a_015 for mother). Informant facts are atomic: name (a_016), relationship (a_017), residence (a_018). Burial similarly bundles date and place as one assertion (reasonable). No compound information crammed inappropriately into single fact_type entries.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Mary Flynn is correctly identified as the named informant on most personal/biographical facts (name, sex, race, age, occupation, birthplace), with appropriate informant_proximity assignments (witness for death facts she could directly report, family_not_present for birth facts she was told about). However, the death assertion (a_005) incorrectly lists Dr. William H. Stein as the sole informant for death date/place/cause/illness, bundling them together. Per the rubric, Mary Flynn is PRIMARY for death date and place (she was present), while Dr. Stein is the informant only for cause of death and duration (he diagnosed it, she reported the event). The skill should have separated these or correctly attributed death date/place to Mary Flynn with informant_proximity: primary. Burial correctly attributes to J.J. Franey (undertaker). Proximity assessments are otherwise sound, but this conflation is a material gap.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Most evidence types are correctly assigned. Death facts (a_005, a_010, a_011) correctly use direct evidence (stated explicitly on the certificate). Age (a_006) is direct; computed birth year (a_007) correctly uses indirect since it is arithmetic-derived. Parents' names and birthplaces (a_012–a_015) are correctly marked indirect—the informant was reporting hearsay/what she was told, not witnessing the parents' births. However, the birthplace assertion (a_008) for Patrick is marked as 'direct' evidence when it should arguably be 'indirect'—Mary Flynn was not present at Patrick's birth in Ireland and reported his birthplace from what she knew/was told, not from direct observation. The informant_proximity field correctly flags family_not_present, creating a mismatch between evidence_type and proximity. This is a minor inconsistency in one assertion's classification.

Let me check the spec and project path before writing.Good. I have the project state:
- `research.json`: empty — `src_001` will be first, no existing log entries
- `tree.gedcomx.json`: one person (I1 = Patrick Flynn), no sources → first source will be `S1`
- 5d sibling stubs: not applicable (subject role is `deceased`, not `child_N`)

Firing log entry and tree source in parallel:Got `log_001` and `S1`. Now persisting the source + all 18 assertions in one batch:All 19 ops validated and persisted. Here's the summary:

---

**Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — Pennsylvania Certificate of Death, Patrick Flynn, 1908 (derivative)

**Assertions by role:**
- `deceased` (Patrick Flynn): **11** — a_001–a_011 (name, sex, race, death date+place, age, birth year, birthplace, occupation, cause of death, illness duration, burial)
- `father_of_deceased` (Thomas Flynn): **2** — a_012–a_013 (name, birthplace)
- `mother_of_deceased` (Mary Brennan): **2** — a_014–a_015 (name, birthplace)
- `informant` (Mary Flynn): **3** — a_016–a_018 (name, relationship, residence)

**Tree changes:** `S1` added; no new persons (5d stubs not applicable — subject role is `deceased`, not `child_N`)

**Key findings:**
- Three informants classified: Mary Flynn (wife) for personal/biographical facts; Dr. William H. Stein for death date/place/cause/illness; J.J. Franey for burial date/place
- Parents' names and birthplaces (a_012–a_015) correctly classified **indirect / secondary** per GPS death-certificate guidance
- Patrick's birthplace (Ireland) and computed birth year (~1845) separated into distinct assertions with different informant-proximity assessments
- Thomas Flynn (father) and Mary Brennan (mother, maiden name) both named — strong leads for Irish origins research

**Next:** Run `check-warnings` on I1, then `person-evidence` to link the `deceased` assertions to I1.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: Mostly correct factual extraction with one material issue: the skill classified the death and burial assertions as containing both multiple informants, but the death entry (a_005) lists Dr. Stein as the informant while Mary Flynn should be named for death date/place (she was present). The rubric context indicates Mary Flynn is the PRIMARY informant for death facts since she was present/aware; the physician only certified cause and duration. The certificate itself names Mary Flynn as informant, not the doctor. This is an unsupported classification that affects downstream conflict resolution. Seventeen of 18 assertions are properly sourced; one critical informant assignment is incorrect.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all significant information from the certificate: 18 assertions covering Patrick Flynn's death facts (11), parents' information (4), and informant data (3). Death and burial dates, places, cause, occupation, birthplace, and computed birth year all captured. No material omissions—the user message provided a complete certificate and the skill processed every relevant field.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All three MCP tool calls used correct arguments matching the expected pattern. research_log_append recorded a positive user-provided source with appropriate query structure. tree_edit added the source with proper title/author metadata. research_append submitted 18 assertions and 1 source entry with valid schema, correct record_id format, proper section structure, and valid operation types. No fixture_not_found errors; all calls matched live fixtures successfully.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact. Death date (a_005) and death place appear together in a single assertion (permissible—closely linked event), but the skill correctly separated age (a_006) from computed birth year (a_007). Parents' names and birthplaces are separate assertions (a_012/a_013 for father; a_014/a_015 for mother). Informant facts are atomic: name (a_016), relationship (a_017), residence (a_018). Burial similarly bundles date and place as one assertion (reasonable). No compound information crammed inappropriately into single fact_type entries.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Mary Flynn is correctly identified as the named informant on most personal/biographical facts (name, sex, race, age, occupation, birthplace), with appropriate informant_proximity assignments (witness for death facts she could directly report, family_not_present for birth facts she was told about). However, the death assertion (a_005) incorrectly lists Dr. William H. Stein as the sole informant for death date/place/cause/illness, bundling them together. Per the rubric, Mary Flynn is PRIMARY for death date and place (she was present), while Dr. Stein is the informant only for cause of death and duration (he diagnosed it, she reported the event). The skill should have separated these or correctly attributed death date/place to Mary Flynn with informant_proximity: primary. Burial correctly attributes to J.J. Franey (undertaker). Proximity assessments are otherwise sound, but this conflation is a material gap.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Most evidence types are correctly assigned. Death facts (a_005, a_010, a_011) correctly use direct evidence (stated explicitly on the certificate). Age (a_006) is direct; computed birth year (a_007) correctly uses indirect since it is arithmetic-derived. Parents' names and birthplaces (a_012–a_015) are correctly marked indirect—the informant was reporting hearsay/what she was told, not witnessing the parents' births. However, the birthplace assertion (a_008) for Patrick is marked as 'direct' evidence when it should arguably be 'indirect'—Mary Flynn was not present at Patrick's birth in Ireland and reported his birthplace from what she knew/was told, not from direct observation. The informant_proximity field correctly flags family_not_present, creating a mismatch between evidence_type and proximity. This is a minor inconsistency in one assertion's classification.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted assertions accurately from the marriage license. All 21 assertions correctly reflect source data with proper evidence types (direct for stated facts, indirect for calculated birth year). Informant identities are accurate—groom and bride for their own facts (proximity 'self'), Rev. O'Connor for the marriage event (proximity 'official_duty'). The birthplace conflict was correctly identified and flagged. No fabricated material.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All required assertions were extracted: both groom and bride received full assertion sets (9 assertions each covering name, age, birth year, birthplace, residence, occupation, parents, and marriage). Witnesses were noted as FAN associates with identifying facts only (4 parent name assertions). All source details were captured in the citation. The conflict with project objective was surfaced.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Three MCP calls were made with correct and complete arguments: (1) research_log_append with accurate query metadata (record_type, county, license number, parties, date); (2) tree_edit add_source with appropriate source title; (3) research_append with 21 correctly structured assertion entries and one source entry. All calls matched expected patterns without errors or fixture_not_found failures.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Every assertion represents a single extractable fact. Name, age, birthplace, occupation, residence, and each parent relationship are separate entries with distinct fact_type values. Birth year (calculated from age) is isolated as its own assertion with evidence_type 'indirect'. Marriage event combines date and place in one assertion (appropriate since they form a single fact), with proper structured_value field. No compound claims crammed into single value fields.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informants are correctly and specifically identified. Patrick Flynn and Catherine Gallagher are named as informants for their own facts with proximity 'self'. For parents (Thomas Flynn, Mary Brennan, James Gallagher, Bridget Dolan), the groom/bride is named as informant with proximity 'family_not_present'—acknowledging the groom/bride reported parental names but the parents were not present. For the marriage event, Rev. Daniel O'Connor (officiant) is the informant with proximity 'official_duty', which is appropriate. Informant bias notes in relevant entries explain context (e.g., Thomas Flynn witness correlation).

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are assigned correctly throughout. Direct evidence: name, age, birthplace, residence, occupation, parental relationships, and marriage date/place—all explicitly stated on the record. Indirect evidence: birth years (~1845, ~1848)—correctly marked as calculated from stated ages with date_certainty 'calculated' and explanatory notes. All classifications align with per-test context expectations. No misassignments that would undermine downstream conflict resolution.

output.text_response:
Logging user-provided record, then loading tool schemas.Logging the user-provided record.Adding the source description to the tree.Now persisting source and all 21 assertions in one batch.**Extraction complete — `src_001` / `S1` · 21 assertions (a_001–a_021)**

| Role | Assertions |
|---|---|
| `groom` (Patrick Flynn) | a_001–a_009: name, age, birth ~1845, birthplace Ireland, residence, occupation, father, mother, marriage |
| `bride` (Catherine Gallagher) | a_010–a_017: name, age, birth ~1848, birthplace PA, residence, father, mother, marriage |
| `father_of_groom` (Thomas Flynn) | a_018: name |
| `mother_of_groom` (Mary Brennan) | a_019: name |
| `father_of_bride` (James Gallagher) | a_020: name |
| `mother_of_bride` (Bridget Dolan) | a_021: name |

**Tree changes:** `S1` added; no person stubs (groom role, not a child-in-household record).

**Key findings:**
- **⚠ Birthplace conflict:** Groom states **Ireland**; the project objective has him born in **Pennsylvania**. Run `conflict-resolution` before closing this question.
- **Parents named:** Thomas Flynn (father) and Mary Brennan (mother) of Patrick Flynn are now on record — directly relevant to the project objective.
- **Witnesses double as parents:** Thomas Flynn (witness_1) = father_of_groom; James Gallagher (witness_2) = father_of_bride — corroborating presence and identity.
- Source classification: **original** (license created at time of event by a county official).

**Next steps:** `person-evidence` to link the groom persona to I1, then `conflict-resolution` for the Ireland vs. Pennsylvania birthplace discrepancy.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted assertions accurately from the marriage license. All 21 assertions correctly reflect source data with proper evidence types (direct for stated facts, indirect for calculated birth year). Informant identities are accurate—groom and bride for their own facts (proximity 'self'), Rev. O'Connor for the marriage event (proximity 'official_duty'). The birthplace conflict was correctly identified and flagged. No fabricated material.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: All required assertions were extracted: both groom and bride received full assertion sets (9 assertions each covering name, age, birth year, birthplace, residence, occupation, parents, and marriage). Witnesses were noted as FAN associates with identifying facts only (4 parent name assertions). All source details were captured in the citation. The conflict with project objective was surfaced.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: Three MCP calls were made with correct and complete arguments: (1) research_log_append with accurate query metadata (record_type, county, license number, parties, date); (2) tree_edit add_source with appropriate source title; (3) research_append with 21 correctly structured assertion entries and one source entry. All calls matched expected patterns without errors or fixture_not_found failures.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Every assertion represents a single extractable fact. Name, age, birthplace, occupation, residence, and each parent relationship are separate entries with distinct fact_type values. Birth year (calculated from age) is isolated as its own assertion with evidence_type 'indirect'. Marriage event combines date and place in one assertion (appropriate since they form a single fact), with proper structured_value field. No compound claims crammed into single value fields.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informants are correctly and specifically identified. Patrick Flynn and Catherine Gallagher are named as informants for their own facts with proximity 'self'. For parents (Thomas Flynn, Mary Brennan, James Gallagher, Bridget Dolan), the groom/bride is named as informant with proximity 'family_not_present'—acknowledging the groom/bride reported parental names but the parents were not present. For the marriage event, Rev. Daniel O'Connor (officiant) is the informant with proximity 'official_duty', which is appropriate. Informant bias notes in relevant entries explain context (e.g., Thomas Flynn witness correlation).

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are assigned correctly throughout. Direct evidence: name, age, birthplace, residence, occupation, parental relationships, and marriage date/place—all explicitly stated on the record. Indirect evidence: birth years (~1845, ~1848)—correctly marked as calculated from stated ages with date_certainty 'calculated' and explanatory notes. All classifications align with per-test context expectations. No misassignments that would undermine downstream conflict resolution.

output.text_response:
I have the full project context. Existing sources run `src_001`–`src_009`, GedcomX sources `S1`–`S8`. This nil search needs: a log entry, a new `S9` source description, a `src_010` source entry, and one negative-evidence assertion.

Step 4 (log entry) and Step 5c (tree source) can fire in parallel:`log_006` and `S9` assigned. Now the batched source + assertion write:All written. Summary:

- **`log_006`** — nil census search logged (1870, Schuylkill County, negative outcome)
- **`S9` / `src_010`** — new derivative source: 1870 U.S. Federal Census, FamilySearch index
- **`a_014`** — negative-evidence assertion: `record_role: absent`, `evidence_type: negative`, `fact_type: residence`, linked to `q_001`

**Key note in `informant_bias_notes`:** the nil is index-level only — mis-transcription, enumerator omission, or relocation could all explain the absence. Browsing the original schedule images page-by-page would yield stronger negative evidence if the gap needs closing.

**Suggested next step:** plan a browse of the original 1870 Schuylkill County images (or a broader county/state search) to harden the negative, then continue with the 1880 and 1900 census searches noted as gaps in `t_001`.

output.text_response:
Reading both project files up front before any extraction work.Both files read. Compiling what I know before touching any tools:

**Project state:** Empty — no sources, assertions, log entries. Subject: I1 = Patrick Flynn (Male).  
**Record:** 1850 U.S. Census, `MXHY-TP4` — Patrick Flynn (P1, child), Thomas Flynn (P2, head/parent).  
**Sibling check (pre-write):** Subject is `child_1`; parent Thomas Flynn is **not** in tree.gedcomx.json → trigger for sibling stubs does not fire (no siblings either). No 5d work needed.  
**Source numbering:** No existing sources → this will be `src_001` / `S1`.

Loading tool schemas now.Schemas loaded. Firing log entry creation and tree source write in parallel — both are independent at this stage.Log `log_001` ✓ | Tree source `S1` ✓. Now batching source + all 7 assertions in one `research_append` call.The validator rejects `record_persona_id` on assertions whose log entry has no sidecar — since search-records handed the record directly without a staged `resultsRef`, I must treat this like a `record_read` and set `record_persona_id: null` on all assertions. Re-batching now.All 8 entries persisted cleanly. Now checking whether FamilySearch persona `MXHY-TP4` is already attached to a tree person.All done. Here is the terse summary:

---

**Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — *1850 U.S. Federal Census*, FamilySearch index (derivative), ARK `MXHY-TP4`

**Assertions written — 7 total:**
- `child_1` (Patrick Flynn / P1): `a_001` name · `a_002` birth year ca. 1845 (indirect — age→year conversion by indexer) · `a_003` birthplace Ireland · `a_004` residence Branch Township, Schuylkill Co., PA · `a_005` relationship child_inferred of head (indirect — no 1850 relationship column)
- `head_of_household` (Thomas Flynn / P2): `a_006` name · `a_007` residence Branch Township

**Tree changes:** `S1` added to `tree.gedcomx.json`. No sibling stubs — Thomas Flynn (potential parent) is not yet in the tree; trigger did not fire.

**Match check:** `MXHY-TP4` is **already accepted** on FamilySearch tree person **LZNY-BRF** "Patrick Flynn" (confidence 5, score 0.97). This is almost certainly our subject I1 — worth noting his FamilySearch PID for future tree-edit work.

**Note on `record_persona_id`:** Set to `null` on all assertions because search-records handed this result without a staged `resultsRef` sidecar; the validator enforces the pairing. If search-records is re-run with `projectPath` to stage the results, assertions can be re-linked with the correct persona IDs (P1/P2) to unlock the `same_person` matcher in person-evidence.

**Key finding:** Thomas Flynn is identified as Patrick's likely father (ca. 1845 Ireland → Schuylkill Co. PA), but the 1850 census relationship is **indirect only** — corroboration needed.

**Next steps:** Run `person-evidence` to formally link `a_001`–`a_005` to I1; then `check-warnings` for plausibility; then search for Thomas Flynn in additional records to establish the parent-child relationship as a proof.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly extracted six atomic assertions from the 1850 census record with appropriate fact types, evidence classifications, informant identifications, and source citations. The birthplace discrepancy with the project objective was properly flagged. The second tool call to record_record_matches correctly retrieved the two pending collateral matches (1860 census confidence 4 and PA death certs confidence 3). No fabricated material; all claims are supported by the provided record and tool responses.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill completed all required work: extracted the 1850 census record (6 assertions spanning name, age, birth year, birthplace, residence, and relationship), added the source with proper citation, logged the search result, and called record_record_matches as instructed to surface the two pending collateral matches. The discrepancy between project objective (Pennsylvania birth) and record evidence (Ireland) was explicitly surfaced. Nothing required was omitted.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All five MCP tool calls passed correct arguments: tree_edit add_source with correct title and URL; research_log_append with record_search tool, collection name, name, arkId, and place; research_append with properly structured sources and assertions payloads (retried after fixing record_persona_id from 'P1' to null per schema constraint); record_record_matches with id='MXHY-TP4' matching the expected_args exactly. All calls matched their fixtures successfully (kind='live' or 'predicate').

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each of the six assertions is a single extractable fact: a_001 name only; a_002 age only; a_003 birth year only (computed separately from age); a_004 birthplace only; a_005 residence with date; a_006 relationship type. No compound assertions; the skill did not group 'age and birth year' or 'birthplace and residence' into single entries. Downstream conflict resolution can query each fact independently.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: For name/age/birthplace (child facts), the skill identified 'unknown household member, likely a parent' with proximity 'household_member' and provided reasoning in informant_bias_notes explaining why (young child could not self-report; parent must have provided). For residence, the skill correctly used 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness' with explanation that enumerator visited the dwelling. Informant was distinguished from recorder in all cases with clear justification.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types were correctly assigned: name, age, and birthplace marked 'direct' (explicitly stated in the record); birth year marked 'indirect' (computed from age minus census year); residence marked 'direct' (enumerator observation); relationship marked 'indirect' (inferred from 1850 household position—no explicit relationship column exists in 1850 census, only from 1880 onward). The skill correctly applied knowledge that 1850 lacks a relationship column and therefore father–child co-residence is indirect evidence.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All assertions are factually accurate. Names, ages, birthplaces, residences, and relationship inferences are correctly extracted from the GedcomX structure and record. Record IDs use the full arkUrl verbatim. Log entry reference (log_001) is correctly cited. Evidence type assignments properly distinguish between direct facts (name, age at census) and indirect inferences (birth year from age calculation, relationships from 1850 household position). No fabricated claims.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All facts in the record were extracted: Patrick's name, age (5), derived birth year (~1845), birthplace (Ireland), residence; Thomas and Mary's names and residences; all three ParentChild relationships from the gedcomx. The skill correctly identified that no siblings appear on the record, so no sibling stubs were created. Referenced the pre-existing log_001 entry as instructed rather than creating a duplicate. Addressed the complete record including all three persons (P1, P2, P3) and all relationships.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Both tool calls used correct, semantically valid arguments. tree_edit call added the source with proper title and collection URL. research_append batched 1 source entry + 12 assertions with all required fields: record_persona_id correctly matched to GedcomX ids (P1, P2, P3); record_id contains the full arkUrl; log_entry_id correctly set to 'log_001' for all entries (not creating a duplicate log entry); fact_types are semantically appropriate (name, age, birth, birthplace, residence, relationship). The batched structure with section/op pairs for each entry is valid JSON. No critical identifier errors; all free-text values match expected content.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion captures a single extractable fact. Name, age, birth year, birthplace, and residence are separate assertions (a_001–a_006 for Patrick). Relationships are decomposed into individual assertions (a_007 to Thomas, a_008 to Mary). Compound information (e.g., 'age 5, born ~1845') was correctly split: age as a_002 with value 'age 5' and date 1850; birth as a_004 with derived year 1845 and indirect evidence type. Thomas and Mary's facts similarly decomposed. Downstream skills can query individual fact_types without parsing compound values.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informants properly identified with reasoning. For Patrick's name, age, birthplace: 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member' and reasoning in informant_bias_notes explaining a 5-year-old could not self-report. For residence (Patrick, Thomas, Mary): 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness'—the enumerator visited the dwelling and observed directly. For Thomas and Mary's names: 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with appropriate household_member proximity. The skill distinguished the recorder (enumerator) from actual informants (household members who reported facts). No conflation between the two roles.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly assigned. Direct evidence applied to: name (explicitly stated), age 5 (directly reported), birthplace Ireland (stated in record), residence (enumerator witnessed it). Indirect evidence correctly applied to: birth year ~1845 (derived by subtracting age from census year, not stated), relationship to Thomas (1850 census lacks relationship column; inferred from household position), relationship to Mary (inferred from position as 'wife' in indexer's household order). Mary's spouse relationship to Thomas also indirect—the 1850 census does not explicitly state marital relationships. Per-test context confirmed 1850 relationships are indirect; the skill accurately applied this rule across all relationship assertions.

output.text_response:
Good — tools loaded. Let me read both files once, then proceed with the full extraction.Good — sidecar confirms standard place: `"Branch Township, Schuylkill, Pennsylvania, United States"`. No other children in the household, so no sibling stub trigger.

**Sibling/parent enumeration (5d check):**
- Parents in tree: Thomas Flynn → not found; Mary Flynn → not found → trigger fails → skip stubs
- Siblings on record: none (Patrick is the only child_N role)

Now executing — tree_edit first to get the `S` id:`S1` assigned. Now the batched `research_append` — source + all 12 assertions in one call:The batched `ops` form still needs top-level `section`/`op`. Let me try with them included alongside `ops`:All 13 entries written and validated. Here's the summary:

---

**Source:** `src_001` → `S1` — *1850 U.S. Federal Census*, derivative, FamilySearch, accessed 9 Jul 2026

**Assertions extracted — 12 total (`a_001`–`a_012`):**

| Role (persona) | Count | Facts |
|---|---|---|
| `child_1` (P1 — Patrick) | 7 | name, age (direct), birth year ~1845 (indirect), birthplace Ireland, residence, relationship→Thomas (indirect), relationship→Mary (indirect) |
| `head_of_household` (P2 — Thomas) | 2 | name, residence |
| `wife` (P3 — Mary) | 3 | name, residence, relationship→Thomas (indirect) |

**Tree changes:** `S1` added; no person stubs (no siblings on record; parents Thomas & Mary not yet in tree — trigger condition unmet).

**Key findings:**
- Patrick born **~1845 in Ireland**, residing Branch Township, Schuylkill, PA in 1850 — consistent with research objective
- Thomas Flynn (head) and Mary Flynn (wife) are probable parents — relationship is **indirect** (1850 census has no relationship column; inferred by indexer from household position)
- Birthplace "Ireland" — no county/parish specificity; a gap for downstream research

**Next steps:** Run `check-warnings` on I1, then `person-evidence` to link P1→I1 and create stubs for Thomas (P2) and Mary (P3).

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All assertions are factually accurate. Names, ages, birthplaces, residences, and relationship inferences are correctly extracted from the GedcomX structure and record. Record IDs use the full arkUrl verbatim. Log entry reference (log_001) is correctly cited. Evidence type assignments properly distinguish between direct facts (name, age at census) and indirect inferences (birth year from age calculation, relationships from 1850 household position). No fabricated claims.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: All facts in the record were extracted: Patrick's name, age (5), derived birth year (~1845), birthplace (Ireland), residence; Thomas and Mary's names and residences; all three ParentChild relationships from the gedcomx. The skill correctly identified that no siblings appear on the record, so no sibling stubs were created. Referenced the pre-existing log_001 entry as instructed rather than creating a duplicate. Addressed the complete record including all three persons (P1, P2, P3) and all relationships.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: Both tool calls used correct, semantically valid arguments. tree_edit call added the source with proper title and collection URL. research_append batched 1 source entry + 12 assertions with all required fields: record_persona_id correctly matched to GedcomX ids (P1, P2, P3); record_id contains the full arkUrl; log_entry_id correctly set to 'log_001' for all entries (not creating a duplicate log entry); fact_types are semantically appropriate (name, age, birth, birthplace, residence, relationship). The batched structure with section/op pairs for each entry is valid JSON. No critical identifier errors; all free-text values match expected content.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion captures a single extractable fact. Name, age, birth year, birthplace, and residence are separate assertions (a_001–a_006 for Patrick). Relationships are decomposed into individual assertions (a_007 to Thomas, a_008 to Mary). Compound information (e.g., 'age 5, born ~1845') was correctly split: age as a_002 with value 'age 5' and date 1850; birth as a_004 with derived year 1845 and indirect evidence type. Thomas and Mary's facts similarly decomposed. Downstream skills can query individual fact_types without parsing compound values.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informants properly identified with reasoning. For Patrick's name, age, birthplace: 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member' and reasoning in informant_bias_notes explaining a 5-year-old could not self-report. For residence (Patrick, Thomas, Mary): 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness'—the enumerator visited the dwelling and observed directly. For Thomas and Mary's names: 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with appropriate household_member proximity. The skill distinguished the recorder (enumerator) from actual informants (household members who reported facts). No conflation between the two roles.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly assigned. Direct evidence applied to: name (explicitly stated), age 5 (directly reported), birthplace Ireland (stated in record), residence (enumerator witnessed it). Indirect evidence correctly applied to: birth year ~1845 (derived by subtracting age from census year, not stated), relationship to Thomas (1850 census lacks relationship column; inferred from household position), relationship to Mary (inferred from position as 'wife' in indexer's household order). Mary's spouse relationship to Thomas also indirect—the 1850 census does not explicitly state marital relationships. Per-test context confirmed 1850 relationships are indirect; the skill accurately applied this rule across all relationship assertions.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly extracted all assertions from the source material and, critically, flagged the suspect father's patronymic 'Nadnesen' as tentative (marked with [?]) in the extracted value rather than recording it as a confident established fact. The skill's reasoning about why 'Nadnesen' is doubtful ('Nadn-' does not match any Norwegian given name; 'Aadnesen' is more plausible) is sound and supported by the source. The other extractions (christening date 20 July 1817 in Stavanger, birth date 11 July 1817, mother Inger Larsdatter, father's residence Birkeland) are accurate. The skill did not commit the error of finalizing the suspect required identifier as established fact.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed the user's core request to extract the father's identity and all directly related facts from the christening record. It extracted the child's christening details (name, christening date, birth date, birthplace), the father's name (with appropriate tentative marking), the father's residence, the mother's name and residence, and captured all relationship facts. The skill also appropriately identified the next step: consulting the original parish register image to confirm the patronymic spelling, and noted this as outstanding work rather than treating it as already resolved.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All four MCP tool calls used correct arguments. The record_read call correctly identified the recordId as 'ark:/61903/1:1:68Q3-5SGC' matching the expected fixture pattern. The research_log_append call recorded the record read with appropriate metadata. The tree_edit call to add the source was correct. The research_append call properly batched all 11 operations (1 source + 10 assertions) with the correct project path and well-formed JSON structure. No tool calls returned fixture_not_found errors.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each of the 10 assertions captures a single extractable fact with its own fact_type. The child's christening is decomposed into separate assertions for name (a_001), christening event (a_002), birth date (a_003), and birthplace (a_004). The father's facts are similarly atomic: name (a_005), residence (a_006), and relationship (a_007). The mother's assertions follow the same pattern: name (a_008), residence (a_009), and relationship (a_010). No compound claims are bundled into single assertions; each field is properly separated.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified the informant as 'Stavanger parish clergy' (the person who recorded the entry, not simply 'census') and distinguished informant_proximity as 'official_duty' for the clergy and 'household_member' for the birth date and birthplace (properly noting that the parents reported these facts to the clergy at christening time). For birth and birthplace, the skill explicitly identified the informant as 'parent(s) of Christian Frederich' with household_member proximity and included notes explaining why parents had first-hand knowledge. This is specific and contextually appropriate.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All evidence types are correctly marked as 'direct': the christening date, birth date, birthplace, and parent names are all explicitly stated in the parish register entry, not inferred from household position or other context. The relationships (father, mother) are also direct evidence because the register explicitly identifies these relationships. The skill correctly applied direct evidence type across all assertions since the source is an original parish register with explicit statements, not a census with inferred relationships.

output.text_response:
Good. Let me read the project files first, then fetch the record.Now fetching the record.Project is empty (no prior sources, no assertions). Now I'll log the record_read and create the GedcomX source description in parallel — I need both IDs before writing the research_append batch.Log `log_001`, source `S1`. Now checking sibling-stub trigger: neither parent exists in the tree (tree has only I1 Patrick Flynn) → trigger condition not met, skip 5d. Writing source + assertions in one batch.All 11 ops persisted (src_001 / S1, a_001–a_010). Here is the summary:

---

**Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — *Norway, Church Books, 1797–1958*, FamilySearch `ark:/61903/1:1:68Q3-5SGC` · classified **original**

**Assertions:** 10 total
- `christened` (Christian Frederich): 4 — name, christening event (20 Jul 1817, Stavanger), birth date (11 Jul 1817), birthplace
- `father`: 3 — name **[tentative]**, residence, relationship
- `mother` (Inger Larsdatter): 3 — name, residence, relationship

---

**Key finding on the father's identity — and why "Nadnesen" must be treated as tentative:**

| Element | Index value | Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| Given name | **Tollev** | ✅ Solid — the child's adult patronymic *Tollefsen* independently confirms his father was named Tollev/Tollef |
| Patronymic | **Nadnesen[?]** | ⚠️ Tentative — "Nadn-" is not a Norwegian given name; no -sen patronymic can be formed from it. Almost certainly a misread of **Aadnesen** (from father named *Aadne*), where the script's Aa loop reads as N |
| Farm | **Birkeland** | ✅ Solid — matches the farm surname the child carried into adult life |

**Working hypothesis:** the father is **Tollev Aadnesen** of the Birkeland farm, Stavanger — but the patronymic remains `[?]` in the record until the original register image is verified.

**Outstanding step:** Delegate to `@plugin:image-reader` with the FamilySearch image group for the 1817 Stavanger register to read the manuscript entry directly and resolve "Nadnesen" vs. "Aadnesen" (or another reading). Once the image confirms the spelling, update `a_005` via `research_append` op `update` and remove the `[?]`.

args.notes: 1817 Stavanger baptism of Christian Frederich (Kristian Fredrik Tollefsen Birkeland). Index transcribes father as 'Tollev Nadnesen' — patronymic 'Nadnesen' is a likely OCR/handwriting misread; 'Nadn-' does not correspond to any attested Norwegian given name from which a patronymic could be formed. Most probable correct reading: 'Aadnesen' (from father named Aadne), where a leading Aa in early-19th-century script can be misread as N. Original parish register image must be consulted to confirm. Mother: Inger Larsdatter. Father's residence listed as Birkeland farm.

citation_detail.what: Christening register entry for Christian Frederich (Kristian Fredrik Tollefsen Birkeland), christened 20 July 1817, born 11 July 1817

added.notes: Digitized original parish church book for Stavanger, Rogaland, Norway. The FamilySearch index transcribes the father's surname as 'Nadnesen.' This is a suspected handwriting misread: in early-19th-century Norwegian script, a leading 'Aa' (double-a) can be misread as 'N', making 'Aadnesen' (patronymic from father named Aadne) the most plausible correct reading. 'Nadn-' does not correspond to any attested Norwegian given name from which a -sen patronymic could be formed. The subject's own adult patronymic 'Tollefsen' (son of Tollev/Tollef) is consistent with the father's given name as indexed. The father's surname must be treated as tentative until the original register image is consulted.

The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly extracted all assertions from the source material and, critically, flagged the suspect father's patronymic 'Nadnesen' as tentative (marked with [?]) in the extracted value rather than recording it as a confident established fact. The skill's reasoning about why 'Nadnesen' is doubtful ('Nadn-' does not match any Norwegian given name; 'Aadnesen' is more plausible) is sound and supported by the source. The other extractions (christening date 20 July 1817 in Stavanger, birth date 11 July 1817, mother Inger Larsdatter, father's residence Birkeland) are accurate. The skill did not commit the error of finalizing the suspect required identifier as established fact.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed the user's core request to extract the father's identity and all directly related facts from the christening record. It extracted the child's christening details (name, christening date, birth date, birthplace), the father's name (with appropriate tentative marking), the father's residence, the mother's name and residence, and captured all relationship facts. The skill also appropriately identified the next step: consulting the original parish register image to confirm the patronymic spelling, and noted this as outstanding work rather than treating it as already resolved.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All four MCP tool calls used correct arguments. The record_read call correctly identified the recordId as 'ark:/61903/1:1:68Q3-5SGC' matching the expected fixture pattern. The research_log_append call recorded the record read with appropriate metadata. The tree_edit call to add the source was correct. The research_append call properly batched all 11 operations (1 source + 10 assertions) with the correct project path and well-formed JSON structure. No tool calls returned fixture_not_found errors.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each of the 10 assertions captures a single extractable fact with its own fact_type. The child's christening is decomposed into separate assertions for name (a_001), christening event (a_002), birth date (a_003), and birthplace (a_004). The father's facts are similarly atomic: name (a_005), residence (a_006), and relationship (a_007). The mother's assertions follow the same pattern: name (a_008), residence (a_009), and relationship (a_010). No compound claims are bundled into single assertions; each field is properly separated.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified the informant as 'Stavanger parish clergy' (the person who recorded the entry, not simply 'census') and distinguished informant_proximity as 'official_duty' for the clergy and 'household_member' for the birth date and birthplace (properly noting that the parents reported these facts to the clergy at christening time). For birth and birthplace, the skill explicitly identified the informant as 'parent(s) of Christian Frederich' with household_member proximity and included notes explaining why parents had first-hand knowledge. This is specific and contextually appropriate.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: All evidence types are correctly marked as 'direct': the christening date, birth date, birthplace, and parent names are all explicitly stated in the parish register entry, not inferred from household position or other context. The relationships (father, mother) are also direct evidence because the register explicitly identifies these relationships. The skill correctly applied direct evidence type across all assertions since the source is an original parish register with explicit statements, not a census with inferred relationships.
